hook: The most complete Portuguese sea-walls in Morocco, raised after the 1471 conquest
facts: Portuguese forces took Asilah in 1471 and rebuilt the walls to resist artillery, shrinking the perimeter for control
The ramparts run a continuous circuit of curtain walls and seaward bastions around the medina
Portugal abandoned the town in 1549-50; Moulay Ismail retook it in 1692
Often called the best-preserved Portuguese fortifications on the Moroccan coast
w: Raised after the Portuguese stormed the town in 1471, then rebuilt squat and thick to swallow cannon fire. Portugal walked away in 1549; Moulay Ismail clawed it back in 1692. The circuit still runs unbroken around the medina, curtain wall to seaward bastion. Empire's masonry, fully intact.

hook: Western Portuguese sea-bastion, the town's celebrated sunset perch over the Atlantic
facts: A Portuguese bastion projecting seaward at the far western end of the medina walls
Stairs descend from near here to the small Lalla Rahma beach
Long regarded as one of the most popular sunset viewpoints on Morocco's Atlantic coast
Also transliterated Caraquia
w: The Portuguese threw this bastion out into the Atlantic at the medina's western tip, a gun platform watching for sails. Now it watches the sun drop instead, and half the town climbs up for it. Stairs nearby fall to a sliver of beach. Old artillery angle, repurposed for the light.

hook: The bandit-rebel Raisuli's clifftop mansion, with a sea terrace wrapped in dark legend
facts: Built around 1909 for Moulay Ahmed er-Raisuni, the rebel who ruled the Jebala as a pasha
Raisuli's men kidnapped Ion Perdicaris in 1904, prompting Roosevelt's 'Perdicaris alive or Raisuli dead'
Interiors carry zellij, carved stucco and painted cedar; a glazed terrace overlooks the rocks
Restored in 1998; now part of the Hassan II cultural centre, mainly open during the festival
legend: Legend says Raisuli made condemned prisoners walk off the terrace to the rocks some thirty metres below
w: Raisuli, the kidnapper-pasha who held Perdicaris in 1904 and made Roosevelt growl 'alive or Raisuli dead,' built himself this clifftop pile around 1909. Zellij, carved cedar, a glazed terrace over the surf. Legend says condemned men were marched off it to the rocks. Brigandry, with sea views.

hook: The main land gate, still wearing the faded Portuguese royal arms over its arch
facts: One of the two principal gates piercing the medina walls, in the mid-southern stretch
A weathered Portuguese coat of arms survives carved above the gateway
The Ahfir souk traditionally crowds the wall beside it
Part of the 15th-16th century Portuguese gateworks, still intact
w: The medina's main door on the land side, and above the arch a Portuguese royal shield still clings on, blunted by five centuries of salt wind. Market stalls press up against the wall beside it. Walk through and the painted lanes swallow you. A conqueror's heraldry, slowly weathering home.

hook: Whitewashed alleys repainted each summer as Morocco's oldest open-air mural project
facts: The Moussem Culturel International d'Asilah began in 1978, founded by Mohamed Benaissa and Mohamed Melehi
Founding artists included Farid Belkahia, Mohamed Chabaa and Mohamed Hamidi
Reckoned the first street-art festival in Morocco and its longest-running public art project
Murals are repainted on the medina walls each August
w: Since 1978 artists have turned the medina's whitewash into canvas every August, a project Melehi and Benaissa dreamed up to drag a fading town back to life. Belkahia, Chabaa and the rest set the template. Each summer brings fresh paint over last year's ghosts. Morocco's longest-running open-air gallery.

hook: Asilah's emblematic Portuguese tower, a blunt sentinel over the old square
facts: A rectangular Portuguese-style bastion, sometimes called the Red Tower (Borj al-Hamra / al-Qamra)
Stands over Place Ibn Khaldoun near the Grand Mosque and the kasbah gate
Among the most prominent surviving towers of the 15th-century fortifications
Treated as a symbol of the town
w: A squat Portuguese keep over Place Ibn Khaldoun, sometimes the Red Tower, sometimes El Kamra, depending who you ask. It anchored the 15th-century defences and somehow became the town's calling card. The square laps at its feet, the mosque minaret just beyond. Blunt, stubborn, unmistakably Portuguese.

hook: The medina's principal mosque, its pale many-sided minaret rising over the inner square
facts: Founded in the era of Moulay Ismail after his 1692 recapture of the town
Distinguished by a white, many-sided (octagonal-type) minaret
Sits on Place Ibn Khaldoun near the kasbah gate (Bab el Kasaba)
Closed to non-Muslims, as with mosques across Morocco
w: The medina's chief mosque, planted on Place Ibn Khaldoun once Moulay Ismail had wrenched the town off the Portuguese in 1692. Its pale, many-sided minaret leans over the square and sets the rhythm of the day in calls to prayer. Doors closed to outsiders; the minaret does the talking.

hook: A Spanish Franciscan church in Hispano-Moorish dress, rare for its Mass in Arabic
facts: Built by Spanish Franciscan friars in 1925, during the Spanish protectorate
Designed in a Hispano-Moorish style blending colonial and Moroccan motifs
Among very few churches in Morocco still permitted to ring bells for Sunday Mass
Run by Franciscan Missionaries of Mary; known for celebrating Mass in Arabic
w: Spanish Franciscans raised this in 1925 under the protectorate, then dressed it in horseshoe arches and Moorish trim so it half-belongs to the medina. One of the rare Moroccan churches still allowed to ring its bells, and rarer still for a Mass said in Arabic. Catholic faith, speaking the local tongue.

hook: A weathered burial ground for a vanished community, marble slabs half-pillaged
facts: Holds roughly 100-150 graves, many laid with marble slabs and some inscribed in Hebrew
Includes a geniza and walls of what may have been a small synagogue structure
Asilah's Jewish quarter centred on a commerce street near the medina
The Kahal synagogue in town was restored and reopened in 2022
w: A hundred-odd graves for a community that has all but gone, marble slabs cracked and some quietly carried off. A geniza survives, and the bones of what may have been a small synagogue. Hebrew lettering fades in the salt air. The Kahal synagogue in town reopened in 2022; out here, only the dead remain.
